title: Saul's Death
note: The Chronicler adds a theological verdict: Saul died for unfaithfulness and consulting a medium.

title: David Anointed King
note: Chronicles omits the 7-year civil war; 'all Israel' anoints David immediately.

title: The Ark Comes to Jerusalem
note: Chronicles adds extensive Levitical detail and David's psalm of thanksgiving.

diff_annotations.explanation: Samuel's version of the Davidic covenant includes a conditional clause about discipline for iniquity. The Chronicler omits this clause entirely, likely because it was historically fulfilled in the exile and the Chronicler is writing to a post-exilic community reestablishing identity without a Davidic king. The omission shapes the Davidic covenant toward pure promise — which intensifies messianic hope rather than past failure.

texts.matthew: When he commits iniquity, I will discipline him with the rod of men
texts.luke: (absent from 1 Chronicles 17:13 parallel)

title: The Davidic Covenant
note: Nearly identical accounts — the covenant is too important to paraphrase.

diff_annotations.explanation: The Chronicler omits the condition about discipline for iniquity that appears in 2 Samuel 7:14. Writing after the exile, the Chronicler reshapes the Davidic covenant as unconditional promise rather than a mixed promise-and-warning. This omission amplifies the messianic dimension of the Davidic line and explains why the Chronicler's David is an idealized figure never shown in moral failure.

texts.matthew: When he commits iniquity, I will discipline him with the rod of men
texts.luke: (absent from 1 Chronicles 17:13)

title: David's Census
note: 'The LORD' (2 Sam) vs 'Satan' (1 Chr) — the most famous divergence between the two histories.

diff_annotations.explanation: Samuel says YHWH incited David; Chronicles says Satan incited David. This is one of the most discussed theological tensions in biblical parallels. The Chronicler, writing later (post-exilic), uses the adversarial figure of 'the satan' (who appears also in Job 1-2 and Zechariah 3) to distance God from directly causing sin. The two accounts represent different ways of narrating divine sovereignty and human moral responsibility.

texts.matthew: The anger of the LORD was kindled against Israel, and he incited David against them
texts.luke: Then Satan stood against Israel and incited David to number Israel

diff_annotations.explanation: The numbers differ significantly between the two accounts. The discrepancy likely reflects different counting methods (whether Levi and Benjamin were included, how military vs. civilian populations were tallied) or textual transmission variance. Ancient census numbers were often rounded and represent administrative categories rather than precise demographic totals.

texts.matthew: Israel: 800,000 valiant men; Judah: 500,000

title: Sennacherib's Invasion
note: Nearly identical parallel accounts of Sennacherib's siege and the angel of the LORD's deliverance of Jerusalem.

title: Hezekiah's Illness and Recovery
note: Isaiah's version includes Hezekiah's psalm of thanksgiving (Isa 38:9–20), absent from Kings.

title: The Fall of Jerusalem
note: Jeremiah 52 is nearly a verbatim copy of 2 Kings 25 — the destruction of Jerusalem, temple, and exile to Babylon.

texts.matthew: Abraham was the father of Isaac... fourteen generations from Abraham to David, fourteen from David to the exile
texts.luke: Jesus... the son of Adam, the son of God

diff_annotations.explanation: Matthew traces Jesus's lineage forward from Abraham through David — a royal, Jewish genealogy establishing Jesus as Israel's Messiah. Luke traces backward from Jesus to Adam and ultimately to God — a universal genealogy establishing Jesus as savior of all humanity. The genealogies diverge after David: Matthew follows the royal line through Solomon; Luke follows through Nathan. Various harmonization theories exist (one tracing Joseph's line, the other Mary's).

title: The Birth of John the Baptist

texts.matthew: An angel of the Lord appeared to him in a dream... to fulfill what the Lord had spoken through the prophet
texts.luke: A decree went out from Caesar Augustus... she wrapped him in cloths and placed him in a manger

diff_annotations.explanation: Matthew tells the birth from Joseph's perspective, emphasizing prophetic fulfillment (Isaiah 7:14). Luke tells it from Mary's perspective, emphasizing the humble circumstances (manger, shepherds) and the historical context (Roman census). Matthew's narrative moves through dreams and angelic warnings; Luke's through songs and public proclamation.

title: The Circumcision and Naming of Jesus
The Boy Jesus at the Temple

diff_annotations.explanation: Matthew frames Jesus's baptism as a deliberate journey to John, establishing John as the forerunner Jesus approaches. Luke subordinates the baptism itself with a participle ('had been baptized') and places the theophany during prayer — characteristic of Luke's portrait of Jesus as a man of prayer.

texts.matthew: Then Jesus came from Galilee to the Jordan to be baptized by John
texts.luke: Now when all the people were baptized, and when Jesus also had been baptized

diff_annotations.explanation: Matthew uniquely includes a dialogue where Jesus justifies his baptism to John. 'Fulfill all righteousness' is a Matthean theological phrase (cf. 5:17) and addresses a christological problem — why would a sinless Jesus need baptism? Luke omits the problem entirely by backgrounding the baptism.

texts.matthew: Let it be so now, for thus it is fitting for us to fulfill all righteousness
texts.luke: (absent)

diff_annotations.explanation: Matthew's third-person form ('This is') makes the voice a public proclamation to the crowd. Mark and Luke's second-person form ('You are') is a direct divine address to Jesus. The difference may reflect different theological interests: Matthew emphasizes public messianic identification; Mark/Luke emphasize the Father-Son relationship.

texts.matthew: This is my beloved Son (third person — public declaration)
texts.luke: You are my beloved Son (second person — private address to Jesus)

title: The Call of Philip and Nathanael

diff_annotations.explanation: Matthew and Luke reverse the order of the second and third temptations. Matthew's climactic order ends with the mountain/kingdom offer, which Jesus refuses with 'Worship the Lord your God only.' Luke's order ends at the temple in Jerusalem, which may reflect Luke's theological emphasis on Jerusalem as the narrative's destination (cf. Luke 9:51).

texts.matthew: 2nd temptation: pinnacle of the temple; 3rd: all kingdoms of the world
texts.luke: 2nd temptation: all kingdoms of the world; 3rd: pinnacle of the temple

diff_annotations.explanation: Matthew concludes with angelic ministry, echoing Elijah in 1 Kings 19:5-8 and Jesus's declaration that he could call on twelve legions of angels (26:53). Luke's ending is more ominous — 'until an opportune time' — pointing forward to the Passion narrative where the devil returns (22:3, entering Judas). Each ending fits its author's larger narrative arc.

texts.matthew: Then the devil left him, and behold, angels came and were ministering to him
texts.luke: departed from him until an opportune time

diff_annotations.explanation: John places a temple cleansing at the beginning of Jesus' ministry; the Synoptics describe one during Passion Week. Scholars debate whether these are two separate events or one event placed differently for theological purposes.

title: Healing at the Pool of Bethesda

diff_annotations.explanation: Luke places the Nazareth rejection at the very start of Jesus' public ministry as a programmatic event; Mark and Matthew place it later during the Galilean ministry. Luke's account includes the sermon content (Isaiah 61) and the attempt to throw Jesus off a cliff, details absent from the other Gospels.

title: Healing a Demoniac in the Synagogue

diff_annotations.explanation: The lists of the Twelve vary slightly across the Gospels. Matthew and Mark list 'Thaddaeus' where Luke has 'Judas son of James.' Luke alone mentions Jesus spending the night in prayer before selecting them.

title: Come to Me, All Who Are Weary
Sermon on the Mount / Sermon on the Plain

diff_annotations.explanation: Matthew adds 'in spirit,' spiritualizing the poverty. Luke's version addresses the literally poor with second-person directness ('you') — and adds corresponding woes against the rich (6:24). Both reflect authentic aspects of Jesus's teaching: Matthew shapes the beatitudes toward interior disposition, Luke toward social reversal. Neither account contradicts the other; each addresses its community's primary need.

texts.matthew: Blessed are the poor in spirit
texts.luke: Blessed are you who are poor

diff_annotations.explanation: Matthew's 'hunger and thirst for righteousness' is a metaphor for moral and spiritual longing. Luke's 'hungry now' again addresses literal physical hunger. The Greek word for righteousness (dikaiosyne) is a Matthean keynote (5:10, 5:20, 6:1, 6:33) absent from Luke's parallel. Luke's 'now' creates a temporal tension — present hunger will be satisfied in God's coming kingdom.

texts.matthew: Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness
texts.luke: Blessed are you who are hungry now

diff_annotations.explanation: Luke uniquely pairs each beatitude with a corresponding woe, creating an antithetical structure. The woes transform the beatitudes into a declaration of cosmic reversal rather than merely ethical instruction. Matthew's Sermon on the Mount contains no woes at this point (though woes appear in ch. 23). The Lukan structure has a closer formal parallel in Old Testament wisdom literature.

texts.matthew: (no corresponding woes)
texts.luke: But woe to you who are rich, for you have received your consolation

title: The Parable of the Hidden Treasure
The Parable of the Pearl of Great Price

diff_annotations.explanation: Matthew's version is fuller and liturgical: the address is 'Our Father in heaven' (Jewish synagogue formulation), and 'your will be done on earth as in heaven' is added. Luke's version is shorter: simply 'Father' (Abba-form, intimate) and no 'will be done' clause. Matthew's context is teaching about prayer in the Sermon on the Mount; Luke's is a response to a disciple's request. The differences suggest independent transmission of a common teaching.

texts.matthew: Our Father in heaven, hallowed be your name. Your kingdom come, your will be done, on earth as it is in heaven
texts.luke: Father, hallowed be your name. Your kingdom come

diff_annotations.explanation: Matthew uses 'debts' (opheilema — a financial/legal metaphor for sin); Luke uses 'sins' (hamartias — the standard theological term). Matthew's condition is past tense ('have forgiven'); Luke's is present ('we forgive'). Matthew's 'debts' language fits his theme of accounting and forgiveness (18:21-35); Luke's 'sins' fits his concern with repentance and pardon.

texts.matthew: Forgive us our debts, as we also have forgiven our debtors
texts.luke: Forgive us our sins, for we ourselves forgive everyone who is indebted to us

diff_annotations.explanation: The doxology is absent from Luke and from the earliest Matthew manuscripts — it is a liturgical addition drawn from 1 Chronicles 29:11-13 and embedded in corporate worship before entering the manuscript tradition. Luke's shorter form may represent an earlier transmission before liturgical expansion. Neither the doxology's presence nor absence affects the prayer's core theology.

texts.matthew: For yours is the kingdom and the power and the glory forever. Amen
texts.luke: (absent)

texts.matthew: A centurion came to him, appealing to him
texts.luke: He sent some elders of the Jews to him, asking him

diff_annotations.explanation: Matthew has the centurion approach Jesus directly; Luke has him send Jewish elders as intermediaries, then friends. Luke's version may reflect a more literal account of ancient patronage customs, while Matthew compresses the delegation into a direct encounter. Both preserve the centurion's remarkable faith declaration.

texts.matthew: Two demon-possessed men coming from the tombs met him
texts.mark: A man with an unclean spirit... who lived among the tombs

diff_annotations.explanation: Matthew mentions two demoniacs; Mark and Luke focus on one. This is a recurring pattern in Matthew (cf. two blind men in 20:30 vs. Mark's one). Matthew may preserve a fuller count while Mark/Luke focus on the spokesman. Mark's account is the most detailed, with vivid description of the man's uncontrollable behavior.

title: Jairus's Daughter and the Bleeding Woman
Parables of the Kingdom (Wheat & Tares, Net)
The Parable of the Mustard Seed

diff_annotations.explanation: Matthew uniquely includes Peter's expanded confession ('Son of the living God'), Jesus's declaration of the Father's revelation, and the Petrine promise ('I will build my church... I will give you the keys of the kingdom'). These three elements are absent from Mark and Luke. The passage has been central to Catholic-Protestant debates about Petrine primacy. Scholars debate whether the expansion reflects Matthew's source, Matthean redaction, or separate tradition.

texts.matthew: You are the Christ, the Son of the living God... I tell you, you are Peter, and on this rock I will build my church
texts.luke: The Christ of God (no divine Sonship; no Petrine promise)

diff_annotations.explanation: Mark's messianic secret command ('tell no one') is common to all three but with variations. Luke avoids specifying what is to be kept secret, while Matthew specifies 'that he was the Christ.' This reflects the Markan theme of the messianic secret (prominent throughout Mark), which Matthew and Luke preserve but handle differently.

texts.matthew: Then he strictly charged the disciples to tell no one that he was the Christ
texts.luke: And he strictly charged and commanded them to tell this to no one

diff_annotations.explanation: Mark provides the most vivid and detailed account, including the father's poignant cry 'I believe; help my unbelief!' (absent from Matthew and Luke) and the disciples' private question about why they could not cast it out.

title: If Your Brother Sins Against You
The Parable of the Unforgiving Servant
The Parable of the Lost Sheep
Jesus at the Feast of Tabernacles

diff_annotations.explanation: This passage (the Pericope Adulterae) is absent from the earliest and most reliable Greek manuscripts. Most modern scholars consider it a later addition, though possibly preserving an authentic tradition about Jesus. It is typically retained in translations with a note about its textual status.

texts.matthew: A man going on a journey entrusted his property to his servants... five talents, two talents, one talent
texts.luke: A man of noble birth went to a distant country to have himself appointed king... ten minas to ten servants

diff_annotations.explanation: Matthew's version involves three servants with different amounts (5, 2, 1 talents — enormous sums). Luke's version has ten servants each given one mina (a modest sum) and adds a political subplot about citizens who oppose the nobleman's kingship. The parables share a common core but serve different purposes: Matthew emphasizes faithful stewardship proportional to ability; Luke adds a warning about rejecting the returning king.

title: The Parable of the Rich Fool

diff_annotations.explanation: Matthew includes this teaching in the Sermon on the Mount; Luke places it during the journey to Jerusalem. This likely reflects the same core teaching delivered on multiple occasions.

title: Healing the Crippled Woman on the Sabbath
The Parable of the Great Banquet

diff_annotations.explanation: Matthew's version is a royal wedding feast with violent consequences for refusal; Luke's is a great supper with polite excuses. Matthew adds the man without a wedding garment. Scholars debate whether these are variants of one parable or two distinct teachings.

title: The Parable of the Lost Coin
The Parable of the Shrewd Manager

diff_annotations.explanation: Matthew includes the 'exception clause' for sexual immorality (porneia) as grounds for divorce; Mark's account has no exception. This difference has generated centuries of theological debate about the absoluteness of Jesus' prohibition.

title: The Parable of the Persistent Widow
The Pharisee and the Tax Collector

diff_annotations.explanation: Matthew mentions both a donkey and a colt to fulfill Zechariah 9:9 literally (treating Hebrew parallelism as referring to two animals). Mark, Luke, and John mention only one animal. This is a classic example of Matthean fulfillment hermeneutic — the citation drives the narrative detail. Luke emphasizes the colt's unridden status, echoing the virgin animals used for sacred purposes (Num 19:2; 1 Sam 6:7).

texts.matthew: Bring a donkey and a colt... they brought the donkey and the colt and put their cloaks on them
texts.luke: A colt on which no one has ever sat

diff_annotations.explanation: Only John specifies 'palm branches' (phoinix), which has led to 'Palm Sunday.' The Synoptics mention garments and leafy branches cut from fields. John also quotes Psalm 118 plus adds 'King of Israel' to the crowd's acclamation — an overtly royal title absent from the Synoptics. John places the triumphal entry after the raising of Lazarus, with a causal link ('because they heard he had done this sign'), which the Synoptics lack.

texts.matthew: (no palms mentioned)
texts.luke: (no palms mentioned)

diff_annotations.explanation: Luke uniquely includes Pharisees asking Jesus to silence the crowd and Jesus's lament over Jerusalem ('Would that you, even you, had known on this day the things that make for peace'). This lament is characteristically Lukan in its grief for Israel's failure to recognize the time of visitation. Matthew's parallel entry narrative ends with the city stirred and asking 'Who is this?'

texts.matthew: (no Pharisee objection or lament)
texts.luke: Some of the Pharisees said, 'Teacher, rebuke your disciples'... And when he drew near and saw the city, he wept over it

texts.matthew: A woman came to him with an alabaster jar of very expensive perfume, which she poured on his head
texts.mark: A woman came with an alabaster jar of very expensive perfume, made of pure nard. She broke the jar and poured the perfume on his head
texts.john: Mary took about a pint of pure nard, an expensive perfume; she poured it on Jesus' feet and wiped his feet with her hair

diff_annotations.explanation: Matthew and Mark describe anointing of the head (a royal/messianic act); John describes anointing of the feet (an act of devotion and humility). John alone identifies the woman as Mary of Bethany and the objector as Judas. Mark adds the vivid detail of breaking the jar. The differences may reflect separate traditions about the same event or distinct memory perspectives.

title: The Cursing of the Fig Tree

texts.luke: Then Satan entered Judas, called Iscariot
texts.matthew: Then one of the Twelve — the one called Judas Iscariot — went to the chief priests

diff_annotations.explanation: Luke alone attributes Judas's betrayal to Satan entering him (22:3), creating a cosmic dimension to the betrayal. This connects to Luke's note that the devil departed from Jesus 'until an opportune time' after the temptation (4:13). Matthew and Mark present Judas's action without supernatural causation, though Matthew uniquely specifies the price: thirty pieces of silver (echoing Zechariah 11:12).

title: The Washing of the Disciples' Feet

diff_annotations.explanation: Luke has a unique double-cup structure (22:17, 22:20), which some manuscripts omit (the 'Western non-interpolation' problem). Paul's tradition in 1 Cor 11:23-26 matches Luke's form more closely than Mark's. The Markan/Matthean pattern (bread-cup) may reflect the form embedded in early Christian worship; Luke's double-cup may preserve a Passover seder structure (the third and fourth Passover cups).

texts.matthew: Bread first (body), then cup (blood) — one cup at the end
texts.luke: Cup first (v.17), then bread (body), then cup again (new covenant in my blood)

diff_annotations.explanation: Matthew/Mark identify the cup as 'blood of the covenant' (echoing Exodus 24:8). Luke and Paul identify the cup as 'the new covenant in my blood' (echoing Jeremiah 31:31). The Lukan/Pauline formula makes the Jeremianic new covenant explicit; the Markan/Matthean formula emphasizes the covenantal blood-ratification pattern. The difference has shaped different traditions of eucharistic theology.

texts.matthew: This is my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many
texts.luke: This cup that is poured out for you is the new covenant in my blood

diff_annotations.explanation: John's Gospel omits the institution of the Eucharist entirely, replacing it with the footwashing (13:1-17) and the Farewell Discourse (chs. 14-17). Most scholars explain this as deliberate Johannine complementarity — John assumes the reader knows the Synoptic account and instead provides the theological interpretation of what the Supper means through Jesus's own extended teaching.

texts.matthew: (Institution of Eucharist present)
texts.luke: (Institution of Eucharist present)

diff_annotations.explanation: All three Synoptics preserve the prayer but with variations. Matthew/Mark use 'if it is possible' (emphasizing divine power); Luke uses 'if you are willing' (emphasizing divine sovereignty). Mark uniquely includes 'Abba, Father' — the Aramaic intimate address preserved in the Greek text. Luke's version is the most direct statement of the will-surrender formula and fits his portrayal of Jesus as ideally obedient and prayerful.

texts.matthew: If it is possible, let this cup pass from me; nevertheless, not as I will, but as you will
texts.luke: Father, if you are willing, remove this cup from me. Nevertheless, not my will, but yours, be done

diff_annotations.explanation: Luke alone records the angelic strengthening and the bloody sweat (though these verses are disputed textually — absent from the two earliest manuscripts). The passage intensifies Jesus's humanity and suffering in ways that troubled some ancient copyists who found it theologically problematic. Luke's interest in Jesus at prayer, in angels, and in Jesus's human vulnerability (cf. 4:2 on genuine hunger) makes Lukan origin plausible.

texts.matthew: (absent)
texts.luke: An angel from heaven appeared to him, strengthening him. And being in agony he prayed more earnestly; and his sweat became like great drops of blood

title: The Parable of the Ten Virgins

texts.john: My kingdom is not of this world... Everyone on the side of truth listens to me. What is truth?
texts.matthew: Are you the king of the Jews? You have said so

diff_annotations.explanation: John provides an extended theological dialogue between Jesus and Pilate about kingship, truth, and authority that is absent from the Synoptics. The Synoptics present a brief exchange; John develops it into a profound exploration of the nature of Jesus's kingdom. Pilate's question 'What is truth?' is one of the most famous lines unique to John's Gospel.

title: The Last Words from the Cross

texts.matthew: My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?
texts.luke: Father, into your hands I commit my spirit

diff_annotations.explanation: The seven last words of Christ are distributed across the four Gospels. Matthew/Mark record only the cry of dereliction (Psalm 22:1). Luke records three sayings: forgiveness for the executioners, promise to the thief, and the commendation of his spirit (Psalm 31:5). John records three: care for his mother, the cry of thirst, and 'It is finished' (tetelestai). Each Gospel's selection reflects its theological portrait of Jesus's death.

title: The Two Thieves on the Cross

diff_annotations.explanation: Matthew and Mark state both criminals reviled Jesus; Luke alone records that one thief repented and received Jesus' promise of paradise. This is not necessarily contradictory — Matthew and Mark may have summarized, while Luke preserved the detailed exchange.

Luke places the tearing of the curtain before Jesus' death; Matthew and Mark place it immediately after. Luke also attributes the darkness to a solar eclipse, while Matthew adds an earthquake and the resurrection of saints — details unique to his account.

Matthew and Mark record the centurion saying 'Truly this was the Son of God,' a christological confession. Luke has 'Certainly this man was innocent (dikaios)' — emphasizing Jesus' righteousness rather than divine identity, consistent with Luke's broader theme of Jesus as the righteous sufferer.

Matthew's commission centers on disciple-making, baptism, and teaching — a fully orbed ecclesial program. Luke/Acts emphasizes proclamation and repentance, with the Spirit's power as the enabler (Acts 1:8). Matthew adds the Trinitarian baptismal formula unique in the Gospels. Mark's (probably secondary) long ending emphasizes signs accompanying the preached word. Each version shapes how its community understood its mission.

texts.matthew: Make disciples of all nations, baptizing... teaching them to observe all that I have commanded
texts.luke: Repentance for the forgiveness of sins should be proclaimed in his name to all nations

diff_annotations.explanation: Matthew closes with the promise of Christ's abiding presence, bookending the Gospel's opening 'Emmanuel, God with us' (1:23). This is the climax of Matthew's theology of presence. Luke's ending focuses on empowerment — stay in Jerusalem, wait for the Spirit. The Spirit in Acts fills the role that the ongoing presence of Christ fills in Matthew.

texts.matthew: I am with you always, to the end of the age
texts.luke: (Stay in the city until you are clothed with power from on high)

title: Jesus Appears at the Sea of Galilee
